questions.question:
When an interrupt occurs, an operating system
A. ignores the interrupt
B. always changes state of interrupted process after processing the interrupt
C. always resumes execution of interrupted process after processing the interrupt
D. may change state of interrupted process to 'blocked' and schedule another process
questions.answer: D. may change state of interrupted process to 'blocked' and schedule another process
questions.to_find: What does an OS do when an interrupt occurs?

understand.plain: When a hardware or software interrupt arrives, the OS saves the current process state and runs the interrupt handler. After the handler finishes, the OS does NOT necessarily return to the interrupted process — it may decide to block that process and run a different one, which is the basis of preemptive multitasking.

keywords.explain: A signal to the CPU that an event needs immediate attention, causing the CPU to stop its current task and run an interrupt handler.
keywords.example: A keyboard keystroke generates a hardware interrupt, causing the OS to read the key.

keywords.explain: A process can be in states like Running, Ready, or Blocked. An interrupt can trigger a state transition.
keywords.example: A process waiting for I/O completion moves from Running to Blocked when the OS services the interrupt.

keywords.explain: The OS can take the CPU away from the running process and give it to another process, often triggered at interrupt time.
keywords.example: Timer interrupt allows the OS to context-switch to a higher-priority process.

understand.visual_alt: Interrupt handling flow: CPU running process P -> interrupt occurs -> save context -> run ISR -> OS scheduler decides: either resume P or schedule another process Q.

given.aim: Identify the correct behavior of an OS when an interrupt occurs.

terms.meaning: A signal that pauses the current CPU execution
terms.connects: Triggers OS interrupt handler which then makes a scheduling decision

terms.meaning: The code that runs in response to an interrupt
terms.example: Disk ISR marks I/O as complete
terms.connects: After ISR, OS scheduler runs to pick the next process

terms.meaning: After handling the interrupt, the OS may resume the interrupted process or switch to another
terms.example: If a higher-priority process became ready, OS preempts current process
terms.connects: This means the OS does NOT always resume the interrupted process

given.plan: Evaluate each option: A is wrong (OS never simply ignores interrupts it accepts). B is wrong ('always changes state' is too strong). C is wrong ('always resumes' is false — preemptive scheduling can switch to another process). D is correct — the OS may block the interrupted process and schedule another.

steps.title: Eliminate options A, B, and C
steps.apply: A: OS never ignores accepted interrupts — it must service them. B: OS does not ALWAYS change the state — it may resume the same process. C: OS does not ALWAYS resume the interrupted process — preemptive scheduling may switch to another process.
steps.note: The keyword 'always' in B and C makes them incorrect since scheduling is context-dependent.

steps.title: Confirm option D
steps.apply: After processing an interrupt, the OS scheduler re-evaluates which process to run. If the interrupt caused a higher-priority process to become ready (e.g., I/O completion), the OS may change the interrupted process's state to 'blocked' (if it was waiting) or 'ready' and then schedule a different process. This is the standard behavior in preemptive OSes.
steps.note: This is the foundation of preemptive multitasking — the OS retains control at interrupt boundaries.

solution.result: D. may change state of interrupted process to 'blocked' and schedule another process

questions.formula_note: Interrupt handling and preemptive scheduling: after servicing an interrupt, the OS scheduler decides which process runs next — it need not be the interrupted process.

questions.question:
Which of the following devices should get higher priority in assigning interrupts?
A. Hard disk
B. Printer
C. Keyboard
D. Floppy disk
questions.to_find: Which device should get the highest interrupt priority among the listed ones?

understand.plain: Different I/O devices have different data transfer rates and buffer sizes. A device that transfers data very fast (high bandwidth) must be serviced quickly before its buffer overflows and data is lost. The hard disk has the highest data transfer rate among the listed devices, so it needs the highest interrupt priority.

keywords.explain: When multiple interrupts occur simultaneously, the device with higher priority is serviced first.
keywords.example: A hard disk interrupt is serviced before a keyboard interrupt if both arrive at the same time.

keywords.explain: The speed at which a device transfers data; higher rate means the device buffer fills faster and needs faster servicing.
keywords.example: Hard disk: ~100 MB/s; Keyboard: a few bytes/second.

understand.visual_alt: Priority comparison: Hard Disk (highest data rate, must be serviced fastest) > Floppy Disk > Printer > Keyboard (slowest, least time-critical).

given.aim: Determine which device requires the highest interrupt priority based on its characteristics.

terms.meaning: Hard disk has the highest data transfer rate (~100s of MB/s) among the listed devices
terms.connects: Its DMA buffer can overflow quickly if not serviced promptly

terms.meaning: Printers are slow output devices, data loss risk is low
Keyboard is the slowest device — generates only a few bytes per keypress
terms.connects: Lowest priority among the options is acceptable

given.plan: Rank devices by their data transfer rate and timing sensitivity. The device with the highest rate and lowest tolerance for delay (risk of buffer overflow/data loss) gets highest priority.

steps.title: Rank devices by data transfer rate
steps.apply: Hard disk: ~100 MB/s (highest). Floppy disk: ~0.5 MB/s. Printer: ~10-100 KB/s. Keyboard: a few bytes/second (lowest). The hard disk transfers data at the fastest rate and has strict timing requirements — if its interrupt is not serviced quickly, the DMA buffer can overflow, causing data corruption.
steps.note: Higher data rate = less time to react = higher interrupt priority needed.

steps.title: Select the device requiring highest priority
steps.apply: Hard disk has the highest data rate and thus requires the fastest interrupt response time. It must get the highest interrupt priority to avoid data loss from buffer overflows.
steps.note: In interrupt priority design, faster devices (with smaller tolerance windows) always get higher priority.

questions.formula_note: Interrupt priority assignment principle: devices with higher data transfer rates and smaller tolerance for delay are assigned higher interrupt priorities to prevent data loss.

questions.question:
Listed below are some operating system abstractions (in the left column) and the hardware components (in the right column):

(A) Thread           1. Interrupt
(B) Virtual address space   2. Memory
(C) File system          3. CPU
(D) Signal           4. Disk

The correct matching is:
A. (A)-2 (B)-4 (C)-3 (D)-1
B. (A)-1 (B)-2 (C)-3 (D)-4
C. (A)-3 (B)-2 (C)-4 (D)-1
D. (A)-4 (B)-1 (C)-2 (D)-3
questions.to_find: Match each OS abstraction to the hardware component it abstracts.

understand.plain: An operating system provides software abstractions over hardware components. A thread is a software abstraction of the CPU (scheduled on the CPU). Virtual address space abstracts physical memory. A file system abstracts the disk (provides named files instead of raw disk sectors). A signal is a software abstraction of hardware interrupts (notifies processes of events).

keywords.explain: A thread is the OS abstraction for CPU execution — it represents a sequence of instructions that gets scheduled on the CPU.
keywords.example: Multiple threads run on one CPU via time-sharing.

keywords.explain: The OS abstraction for physical memory — each process sees a private, large address space regardless of actual RAM.
keywords.example: A 32-bit process sees 4 GB of virtual memory even if only 1 GB of RAM exists.

keywords.explain: A software mechanism to notify a process of an event — analogous to a hardware interrupt but in software.
keywords.example: SIGKILL sends a signal to a process, similar to an interrupt telling it to terminate.

understand.visual_alt: Matching table: Thread -> CPU (3), Virtual Address Space -> Memory (2), File System -> Disk (4), Signal -> Interrupt (1).

given.aim: Match OS abstractions to the hardware components they abstract over.

terms.meaning: A thread is the abstraction of the CPU — threads are scheduled on CPUs
terms.example: The OS scheduler maps threads to CPU cores

terms.meaning: Virtual address space is the abstraction of physical RAM — the OS creates the illusion of private memory per process
terms.example: Page tables map virtual addresses to physical RAM frames

terms.meaning: File system is the abstraction of disk storage — organizes raw sectors into files and directories
terms.example: ext4, NTFS manage disk blocks as files

terms.meaning: Signal is the software analog of a hardware interrupt — notifies a process asynchronously
terms.example: SIGTERM is like a software interrupt to terminate a process

given.plan: Map each OS concept to its underlying hardware by understanding what hardware resource the OS concept virtualizes or abstracts.

steps.title: Identify each abstraction-hardware pair
steps.apply: (A) Thread: The OS abstracts the CPU as threads — threads get scheduled on CPUs. So Thread -> CPU (3). (B) Virtual address space: The OS abstracts physical memory as a virtual address space. So VAS -> Memory (2). (C) File system: The OS abstracts the disk (raw sectors) as a file system with files and directories. So File system -> Disk (4). (D) Signal: Signals are software notifications analogous to hardware interrupts. So Signal -> Interrupt (1).
steps.note: The key insight is that each OS abstraction hides the complexity of its corresponding hardware resource.

steps.title: Select the matching option
steps.apply: Mapping: (A)-3, (B)-2, (C)-4, (D)-1. This corresponds to option C: (A)-3 (B)-2 (C)-4 (D)-1.
steps.note: Option C is the only one with Thread->CPU(3), VAS->Memory(2), FS->Disk(4), Signal->Interrupt(1).

questions.formula_note: OS abstraction layers: Thread abstracts CPU, Virtual address space abstracts Memory, File system abstracts Disk, Signal abstracts hardware Interrupt.

questions.question:
A processor needs software interrupt to
A. test the interrupt system of the processor
B. implement co-routines
C. obtain system services which need execution of privileged instructions
D. return from subroutine
questions.answer: C. obtain system services which need execution of privileged instructions
questions.to_find: What is the primary purpose of a software interrupt (trap/system call) in a processor?

understand.plain: A software interrupt (also called a trap or system call) is a deliberate instruction executed by a user program to request the OS kernel to perform a privileged operation on its behalf. User programs run in unprivileged mode and cannot directly execute privileged instructions (like I/O operations), so they use a software interrupt to switch to kernel mode and request OS services.

keywords.explain: An intentional interrupt triggered by a specific instruction (INT, TRAP, SYSCALL) that causes the CPU to switch from user mode to kernel mode.
keywords.example: The INT 80h instruction in x86 Linux triggers a system call.

keywords.explain: Instructions that can only be executed in kernel mode (e.g., I/O operations, modifying page tables, halting the CPU).
keywords.example: A user program cannot directly execute IN/OUT instructions; it must use a system call.

keywords.explain: A mechanism for user programs to request services from the OS kernel via a software interrupt.
keywords.example: read(), write(), fork() in Unix are system calls implemented via software interrupts.

understand.visual_alt: User program issues software interrupt (INT/SYSCALL) -> CPU switches to kernel mode -> OS executes privileged instruction on behalf of user -> returns result to user program.

given.aim: Identify why a processor uses software interrupts.

terms.meaning: User mode restricts what instructions a program can execute; kernel mode allows all instructions
terms.example: File I/O requires kernel mode; user code runs in user mode
terms.connects: Software interrupt is the gateway from user mode to kernel mode

terms.meaning: An instruction (like INT n, TRAP, SVC) that causes a controlled switch to kernel mode
terms.example: INT 80h in Linux x86 for system calls
terms.connects: Enables user programs to request OS services

terms.meaning: Operations only the OS kernel can perform (I/O, memory management, etc.)
terms.example: Opening a file requires kernel-level disk access
terms.connects: User program must use software interrupt to invoke these

given.plan: Analyze what unique capability software interrupts provide: the controlled transition from user mode to kernel mode to execute privileged instructions.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply: A: Testing the interrupt system uses hardware test mechanisms, not software interrupts specifically. B: Co-routines are implemented using cooperative scheduling/context switching, not software interrupts. D: Returning from a subroutine uses a RET instruction, not a software interrupt.
steps.note: Options A, B, D describe mechanisms that do not specifically require software interrupts.

steps.title: Confirm option C
steps.apply: A software interrupt (trap) is the standard mechanism for a user-mode program to request OS kernel services that require execution of privileged instructions. When the user program executes INT/TRAP/SYSCALL, the CPU switches to kernel mode, the OS handler runs the privileged instruction, and control returns to the user program. This is the fundamental reason software interrupts exist.
steps.note: This is the definition and primary purpose of a system call / software interrupt in any protected-mode OS.

solution.result: C. obtain system services which need execution of privileged instructions

questions.formula_note: Software interrupt (trap/system call): mechanism for user-mode programs to switch to kernel mode and invoke privileged OS services; fundamental to protected-mode OS design.

questions.question:
A computer handles several interrupt sources of which the following are relevant for this question.
- Interrupt from CPU temperature sensor (raises interrupt if CPU temperature is too high)
- Interrupt from Mouse (raises interrupt if the mouse is moved or a button is pressed)
- Interrupt from Keyboard (raises interrupt if a key is pressed or released)
- Interrupt from Hard Disk (raises interrupt if a disk read or write is completed)

Which one of the following statements is TRUE?
A. Interrupt from Hard Disk must be given the highest priority
B. Interrupt from CPU temperature sensor must be given the highest priority
questions.answer: B. Interrupt from CPU temperature sensor must be given the highest priority
questions.to_find: Which interrupt source should be given the highest priority among the listed devices?

understand.plain: Different interrupt sources have different urgency levels. The CPU temperature sensor raises an interrupt when the CPU is overheating — this is a critical hardware safety condition that must be addressed immediately to prevent permanent hardware damage. No other listed interrupt is as time-critical or potentially destructive if delayed.

keywords.explain: When multiple interrupts arrive simultaneously, the one with highest priority is serviced first.
keywords.example: A power failure interrupt is serviced before a keyboard interrupt.

keywords.explain: Raised when CPU temperature exceeds a safe threshold — requires immediate action (throttling or shutdown) to prevent hardware damage.
keywords.example: If CPU hits 100 degrees C, the sensor interrupt fires; delay could burn out the processor.

keywords.explain: Raised when a disk operation completes — important for performance but a short delay causes no hardware damage.
keywords.example: Disk read completion interrupt — data is waiting in buffer, a small delay is acceptable.

understand.visual_alt: Priority ranking (highest to lowest): CPU Temperature Sensor (critical safety) > Hard Disk (performance-critical) > Keyboard > Mouse.

given.aim: Determine which interrupt must be given the highest priority to ensure system safety.

terms.meaning: If CPU temperature interrupt is delayed, the CPU may be permanently damaged
terms.example: Thermal throttling or emergency shutdown must happen within milliseconds of overheating
terms.connects: Makes CPU temperature sensor interrupt the most time-critical

terms.meaning: Delay in servicing disk interrupt stalls the process waiting for I/O but causes no hardware damage
terms.example: Process waits a few extra microseconds — no permanent damage
terms.connects: High priority but not as critical as temperature sensor

terms.meaning: These are low-bandwidth human-speed inputs; delay of a few milliseconds is imperceptible
terms.example: A 10ms delay in keyboard interrupt is unnoticeable to the user

given.plan: Evaluate each interrupt by its urgency and consequence of delay. Safety-critical interrupts (temperature sensor) take absolute highest priority because delay can cause irreversible hardware damage.

steps.title: Classify interrupts by urgency and consequence
steps.apply: CPU temperature sensor: Safety-critical — overheating can permanently damage the CPU within milliseconds. Action must be immediate (throttle frequency, emergency shutdown). Hard disk: Performance-critical — delay stalls I/O-waiting processes but causes no hardware damage. Mouse/Keyboard: Human-speed I/O — delays of even 10-50ms are imperceptible.
steps.note: Safety-critical > performance-critical > human-speed in interrupt priority design.

steps.title: Determine highest priority interrupt
steps.apply: The CPU temperature sensor interrupt must have the highest priority because: (1) it represents an imminent hardware safety hazard, (2) the consequence of delay is permanent CPU damage — there is no recovery, and (3) the response (thermal throttle or shutdown) must happen within microseconds to milliseconds. Hard disk interrupt (option A) is important for performance but a short delay causes no irreversible damage.
steps.note: In real systems, NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) is often used for such critical hardware safety events.

solution.result: B. Interrupt from CPU temperature sensor must be given the highest priority

questions.formula_note: Interrupt priority assignment: safety-critical interrupts (e.g., thermal protection) must receive highest priority to prevent irreversible hardware damage; this supersedes even I/O performance priorities.
